# Deployment & SEO Configuration

## Deployment Model
- Type: Single Page Application (SPA)
- Hosting: Static (Netlify/Vercel/S3)
- Routing: Client-side via Query Parameters (Hash/History API fallback handled by App.tsx)

## Environment Variables
- NODE_ENV: Set to 'production' for builds to disable debug logging and hide error stack traces.
- Note: Runtime AI features have been decommissioned for v1; no API keys are required for deployment.

## Robots.txt Configuration
User-agent: *
Allow: /
Disallow: /?view=ADMIN_ACCESS
Disallow: /?view=INTERNAL_LOG
Sitemap: https://guynode.gy/sitemap.xml

## Sitemap.xml Structure (Static Generation Recommended)
- https://guynode.gy/
- https://guynode.gy/?view=CATALOG
- https://guynode.gy/?view=MAP
- https://guynode.gy/?view=BLOG_INDEX
- https://guynode.gy/?view=ABOUT
- https://guynode.gy/?view=DOCS

## Build Notes
- External GeoJSON URLs in the catalog may require CORS proxies if they do not send Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers.
- The Admin Console is hidden behind a client-side soft gate. Do not rely on it for securing sensitive data in production.

## Migration & Redirect Strategy
(Recommended for replacing legacy sites)

If deploying this SPA to replace an existing multi-page site, configure your host (Netlify _redirects or Vercel config) to map old paths to new query params.

Examples:
- Old: /data/mining-concessions.html
  New: /?view=CATALOG&category=Economy

- Old: /maps/viewer.php
  New: /?view=MAP

- Old: /about-us
  New: /?view=ABOUT

Ensure "Beta" messaging is active in App.tsx during the transition period.

**4) Alignment Summary**

All runtime AI usage has been successfully decommissioned. 
*   The `@google/genai` library import has been removed from `index.html`.
*   The `geminiService.ts` module has been neutralized to prevent any external API calls or API key access.
*   The `<Assistant />` component has been removed from the main application view (`App.tsx`) and the component file itself has been neutralized.
*   UI triggers for AI features (buttons in Catalog and Viewer) have been removed.
*   Documentation and Privacy Policy have been updated to reflect the static nature of the site.
*   **Result:** The application now runs as a purely static front-end SPA with zero dependencies on external LLM services or API keys.

**5) Logging Entries**

**Internal Dev Log**
```json
{
  "area": "AI Decommissioning",
  "severity": "info",
  "title": "Removed runtime Gemini integration and AI UI triggers",
  "summary": "Decommissioned all client-side AI features to align with v1 static portal goals.",
  "details": [
    "Removed @google/genai from import map in index.html.",
    "Neutralized services/geminiService.ts (removed imports and logic).",
    "Removed <Assistant /> from App.tsx and neutralized the component file.",
    "Removed 'Generate AI Summary' buttons from CatalogCard and ViewerSidebar.",
    "Updated Support/Privacy page to confirm no AI runtime processing.",
    "Removed API_KEY requirement from deployment docs."
  ]
}
```

**Public Changelog**
```json
{
  "area": "Architecture",
  "version": "unreleased",
  "title": "Streamlined interface and removed experimental features",
  "summary": "We've removed experimental AI-powered summaries and assistant features to keep the Guynode portal lightweight, fast, and focused on stable data access.",
  "breakingChange": false
}
```

**6) Verification Checklist**

- [ ] App builds successfully with no references to `@google/genai`.
- [ ] App runs in the browser without any AI-related runtime errors or missing API keys.
- [ ] No “Assistant” UI is visible anywhere in the app.
- [ ] No “Generate AI Summary” or “AI Insight” buttons/sections appear in Catalog or Viewer.
- [ ] Network tab (DevTools) shows no requests to AI/LLM endpoints when using the app.
- [ ] Support/Privacy text confirms no live AI processing of user data.
- [ ] Deployment notes no longer reference AI API keys.
- [ ] No new runtime network calls were introduced.

GUYNODE TASK: WIRE “ADMINISTRATIVE → TERRITORIAL EXTENT” DATASETS INTO SPA

You are operating under the “GUYNODE MASTER INSTRUCTION FILE (v3)” and Guardian Layer. Obey that protocol first, then this task.

MODE:
- EXECUTION mode – you ARE allowed to modify code.
- Scope is LIMITED to:
  - Defining dataset metadata for “Administrative Layers & Maps – Guyana → Territorial Extent”
  - Wiring those datasets into the Catalog
  - Adding safe, minimal “View” vs “Download” behavior for assets based on asset type
- DO NOT introduce new frameworks or routing systems.
- DO NOT add any runtime AI usage, AI libraries, or external network calls beyond what already exists.

REFERENCE PAGE FOR DATA:
- Use this public page as the authoritative source for the Territorial Extent entries:
  - https://guynode.com/admin_boundaries.html#gsc.tab=0
- Focus ONLY on the section:
  - “ADMINISTRATIVE LAYERS AND MAPS – GUYANA → Territorial Extent”
- For each item in that section, extract:
  - Title
  - Short description / notes
  - File types (shapefile, PDF, image, external web map, etc.)
  - File URLs
  - Source (e.g., GLSC, OpenStreetMap, Marine Regions, etc.)
  - Any obvious licence/usage text present on the page

====================================================
TASK 0 – LOCATE EXISTING TYPES & REGISTRIES
====================================================
Goal:
- Find the existing dataset / catalog types and the place where administrative datasets are registered, WITHOUT guessing filenames up front.

0.1 Locate dataset & asset types
- Search the provided code for the main dataset types. Likely names include:
  - `Dataset`, `DatasetMetadata`, `CatalogDataset`, `DatasetAsset`, etc.
- [CONFIRM] the “source of truth” type(s) used across the Catalog and Viewer.
- DO NOT introduce a new, parallel dataset type. You MUST adapt to the existing types.

0.2 Locate administrative dataset registry
- Find the module(s) where datasets for “Administrative” or similar categories are declared.
  - Examples of likely paths (FOR REFERENCE ONLY – DO NOT ASSUME):
    - `src/data/datasets/admin.ts`
    - `src/data/datasets/administrative.ts`
    - `src/data/catalog/admin.ts`
- Identify where Territorial or Administrative entries would belong in this registry.

0.3 Locate Catalog + Viewer integration points
- Find:
  - The component that renders catalog sections / dataset lists (e.g. `CatalogView`, `DatasetList`, etc.).
  - The place where clicking a dataset routes into the Viewer (e.g. a handler like `onOpenDataset` or a link that passes a `datasetId` / query param).
  - Any existing logic that handles “assets” (multiple files per dataset, if present).

0.4 Confidence mapping
- For each of the items above, label them as:
  - [CONFIRMED] – actual types/registries/components used in the running app.
  - [GUESSED] – plausible but not clearly the real one.
- You may ONLY modify [CONFIRMED] modules.
- If something is only [GUESSED], describe what you would do there but DO NOT change it.

OUTPUT (for Task 0):
- A short mapping list:
  - Dataset type: [CONFIRMED] – <File/TypeName>
  - Asset type: [CONFIRMED]/[GUESSED] – <File/TypeName>
  - Admin dataset registry: [CONFIRMED]/[GUESSED] – <File>
  - Catalog view component: [CONFIRMED]/[GUESSED] – <File/Component>
  - Viewer hook for datasets: [CONFIRMED]/[GUESSED] – <File/Component>

====================================================
TASK 1 – DEFINE TERRITORIAL EXTENT DATASETS (DATA MODULE)
====================================================
Goal:
- Create structured dataset entries for each “Territorial Extent” item based on the live Guynode page and align them with the existing dataset type.

Requirements:
- REUSE the existing dataset/asset interfaces discovered in Task 0.
- DO NOT change global types unless absolutely necessary; if you must extend them, keep the change minimal and backward compatible.
- Where the existing type does not support something like `assets[]` or `originalUrl`, propose the smallest compatible addition.

1.1 Create or extend a data module
- If there is an existing pattern (e.g. `src/data/datasets/administrative.ts`), follow it:
  - Add a new exported constant/array for Territorial Extent, e.g. `administrativeTerritorialExtentDatasets`.
- If there is a clear “per-category” module pattern, follow that instead.
- This module MUST:
  - Use the existing dataset type.
  - Export an array of datasets for each Territorial Extent item from the reference page.

1.2 Populate datasets from the live page
For each Territorial Extent item on `admin_boundaries.html`:

- Create ONE dataset record that groups all related formats (shapefile, PDF, image, external map) into that dataset’s assets.
  - The dataset-level fields should include:
    - Title
    - Category (e.g. `"Administrative Layers & Maps – Guyana"`, or your app’s canonical category key)
    - Subcategory (e.g. `"Territorial Extent"`)
    - Short description
    - Source + source URL (if obvious)
    - Any licence hints from the page
    - Relevant tags (`["boundary", "national", "EEZ", "outline-map", ...]`)
  - For each file/format, create an asset entry:
    - `type`: `"shapefile" | "pdf" | "image" | "web-map" | <existing type enums>`
    - `label`: human-readable, e.g. `"National boundary (shapefile, zipped)"`
    - `originalUrl`: the current `https://guynode.com/...` or external URL
    - `storagePath`: set to `null` or a placeholder; DO NOT guess new storage URLs.
    - `isDownloadable: true` for all real files
    - `isViewable`:
      - `true` for PDFs and images (browser/native viewing)
      - For shapefiles: set `isViewable: true` **only if** the existing Viewer workflow already supports a mapped/derived asset; otherwise, set `isViewable: false` and add a comment in the dataset object: `// TODO: connect derived GeoJSON/WMS for map preview`.

- IMPORTANT:
  - Preserve a field for the legacy URL (e.g. `originalUrl`) so humans can later rehost the files and update `storagePath` without losing provenance.
  - Do NOT attempt to convert shapefile URLs into GeoJSON; that is an offline pipeline step.

1.3 Viewer defaults (if supported by existing types)
- If the existing dataset type supports a “default asset” / “viewer config” field:
  - Set the default to:
    - Shapefile or service asset for map-centric datasets.
    - PDF or image asset for purely illustrative/reference datasets.
- If there is no such field, do not invent one; just focus on assets.

OUTPUT (for Task 1):
- The full contents of the new or updated data module that defines the Territorial Extent datasets, using the actual type names and shapes from the codebase.

====================================================
TASK 2 – WIRE TERRITORIAL EXTENT INTO CATALOG
====================================================
Goal:
- Make the new Territorial Extent datasets appear in the Catalog under the correct category/subcategory.

2.1 Register datasets in the administrative catalog registry
- In the [CONFIRMED] admin dataset registry module from Task 0:
  - Import the Territorial Extent dataset array from Task 1.
  - Add it to the relevant category/subcategory data structure so that:
    - The Catalog displays “Administrative Layers & Maps – Guyana”
    - Under it, a sub-section or filter “Territorial Extent”
    - And the new datasets appear there.

2.2 Ensure filters/subcategory labels are correct
- If the Catalog uses enums or constant keys for subcategories:
  - Add or reuse a key for `"Territorial Extent"` consistent with existing patterns.
- Do NOT rename existing categories unless clearly necessary.

OUTPUT (for Task 2):
- The updated admin catalog registry file, showing:
  - The new import
  - The updated registration / array that includes the Territorial Extent datasets.

====================================================
TASK 3 – VIEW vs DOWNLOAD BEHAVIOR FOR ASSETS
====================================================
Goal:
- Provide a minimal, predictable behavior for assets so that all Territorial Extent files are both:
  - **Downloadable**, and
  - **Viewable** where feasible (PDFs & images now; shapefiles as future enhancement).

3.1 Locate the asset-click handler(s)
- Find the [CONFIRMED] place where:
  - A dataset “asset” is rendered with buttons/actions, OR
  - The Catalog or a detail panel triggers viewing/downloading of a file.

3.2 Implement minimal `asset.type` branching
Without breaking existing functionality:

- Add logic similar to:

  - If `asset.type` is `"pdf"`:
    - “View” → `window.open(asset.storagePath ?? asset.originalUrl, '_blank', 'noopener')`
    - “Download” → same URL with `download` attribute if using an `<a>` element.

  - If `asset.type` is `"image"`:
    - “View” → open image in new tab or a simple modal viewer using existing design patterns.
    - “Download” → direct download link.

  - If `asset.type` is `"shapefile"`:
    - For now:
      - “Download” → direct download link.
      - “View”: 
        - EITHER:
          - Hidden/disabled until a derived map-friendly layer is available, OR
          - Shows a simple tooltip/note like “Open this shapefile in a GIS application to view.”
        - DO NOT attempt to parse shapefiles client-side unless the existing Viewer already supports a path for this.

- The goal is to:
  - Ensure **every asset** has a clear download action.
  - Make PDFs/images trivially viewable.
  - Leave shapefile “viewing” as an explicit TODO tied to future GeoJSON/WMS pipelines, not a broken button.

3.3 Do NOT break existing Viewer routing
- If clicking a dataset currently routes into the Leaflet Viewer with `datasetId` or similar:
  - Preserve this behavior.
  - The asset “View”/“Download” behavior should be additive and not interfere with existing map navigation.

OUTPUT (for Task 3):
- The updated component(s) that render dataset assets and handle “View”/“Download” actions, with the new branching logic.

====================================================
TASK 4 – SUMMARY & LOGGING
====================================================
Goal:
- Summarize changes and comply with the Guynode logging mandate.

4.1 Implementation summary
- Provide a short narrative summary describing:
  - Where the Territorial Extent datasets are defined.
  - How they are wired into the Catalog.
  - How asset view/download behavior now works.

**AUDIT REPORT: GUYNODE v3 (React + TypeScript SPA)**

**Date:** October 26, 2025
**Scope:** Comprehensive End-to-End Application Audit
**Goal:** Assess readiness for production deployment to replace the existing Guynode website.

---

### **1. Executive Summary**

The application is architected as a **Static Single Page Application (SPA)** using React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS. It mimics the behavior of a sophisticated full-stack platform (with an "Admin Console," "Ingestion Pipeline," and "AI Assistant") but currently operates entirely client-side.

**Current Status:** **High-Fidelity Prototype / Alpha.**
The application structure, UI/UX, routing logic, and component hierarchy are production-grade. However, the **Data Layer** is currently mocked or embedded directly in the JavaScript bundle. You cannot deploy this *as is* to replace a live data portal without migrating the mock data strategies to real static asset hosting.

**Deploy-ability Score:** **6/10**
(High scores for UI/UX and Architecture; Low scores for Data Persistence and Asset Management).

---

### **2. Detailed Subsystem Audit**

#### **A. Core Architecture & Routing**
*   **State:** **Excellent.**
*   **Findings:**
    *   The app uses a custom `ViewState` routing mechanism (`App.tsx` + `utils/routing.ts`) that manipulates URL query parameters (`?view=MAP&datasetId=...`).
    *   **Pro:** This is perfectly suited for static hosting (Netlify, Vercel, S3) as it avoids server-side routing complexities. Deep linking works correctly.
    *   **Con:** It eschews standard libraries like `react-router`, meaning history management and back-button behavior relies on your custom implementation in `App.tsx`.
*   **SEO:** The `MetaManager` component correctly updates document titles and meta tags dynamically based on the view, which is critical for a public-facing replacement site.

#### **B. Data Layer (The Critical Path)**
*   **State:** **Mock / Embedded.**
*   **Findings:**
    *   **Catalog Metadata:** Sourced from `types.ts` (`MOCK_DATASETS`). This file functions as your "database." To update the site, a developer must edit this file and redeploy.
    *   **Geospatial Data:** Currently stored in `data/geoJsonData.ts` as a massive JavaScript object (`GLOBAL_GEOJSON_DB`).
    *   **Critical Issue:** Storing GIS data (GeoJSON) inside a TypeScript file means the data is bundled into the main application JavaScript.
        *   *Impact:* As soon as you add real datasets (MBs in size), the initial page load time will skyrocket, crashing the browser or causing massive delays.
    *   **Blog & Analysis:** Content is stored in `data/blogPosts.ts` and `data/analyses.ts`. This "Git-based CMS" approach is valid and performant for text content.

#### **C. GIS Viewer Subsystem**
*   **State:** **Feature-Rich, Data-Constrained.**
*   **Findings:**
    *   The `MapViewer` component is robust, featuring layer control, comparison modes, and timeline sliders.
    *   **Leaflet Integration:** Correctly implemented with `useEffect` hooks.
    *   **Performance Logic:** The app contains sophisticated logic for "Progressive Loading" and "Web Workers" (`utils/geojsonLoader.ts`, `workers/geojsonParserWorker.js`). However, the loader currently looks for data in the `GLOBAL_GEOJSON_DB` (memory) rather than fetching external files.
    *   **Styles:** Styling logic is centralized in the dataset config, which is a good pattern.

#### **D. Admin Toolkit & "Backend" Simulation**
*   **State:** **Client-Side Simulation.**
*   **Findings:**
    *   The `AdminConsole` is an impressive suite of tools (Link Checker, Drift Detection, Ingestion).
    *   **Ingestion Pipeline:** The `DatasetUploadPage` allows you to upload a file and validate it. It produces a JSON snippet.
    *   **The Gap:** Because there is no backend API, clicking "Save" or "Ingest" **does not persist data**. It generates code snippets that a developer must manually copy-paste into the codebase (`data/datasets.ts`). This is a "Developer Tool," not a "Content Manager."
    *   **Security:** The login (`AdminAccessPage`) uses a hardcoded password (`guylegend`) checked in the browser. This is strictly "security through obscurity" and serves only to hide the menu from casual visitors.

#### **E. AI Integration**
*   **State:** **Production Ready (Requires Config).**
*   **Findings:**
    *   The `geminiService.ts` is correctly set up to use Google's GenAI SDK.
    *   It relies on `process.env.API_KEY`. You must ensure this environment variable is injected during the build process on your hosting provider.

---

### **3. Deployment Blockers**

Before this application can replace `guynode.gy`, the following **Blocking Issues** must be resolved:

1.  **Externalize GeoJSON Assets:**
    *   You cannot ship `data/geoJsonData.ts` with real data.
    *   Real GeoJSON files must be moved to a `/public/data/` directory or an external object storage bucket (AWS S3, Cloudflare R2).
    *   The `geojsonUrl` in your datasets must point to these fetchable URLs (e.g., `/data/boundaries/region4.geojson`) instead of looking up internal keys.

2.  **Replace Placeholder Images:**
    *   The app heavily uses `picsum.photos` (random image generator). These must be replaced with static assets representing actual Guyana maps/reports to look professional.

3.  **Migration of Legacy Data:**
    *   The `MOCK_DATASETS` array currently contains example data. You need to perform a "Content Entry" phase where you populate this array with the actual inventory of the current Guynode website.

---

### **4. Recommendations & Roadmap**

To move from **Prototype** to **Production**, follow this 3-Phase Plan:

#### **Phase 1: Architecture Refactoring (Data Separation)**
*   **Action:** Delete `data/geoJsonData.ts`.
*   **Action:** Create a folder structure in `public/` (e.g., `public/datasets/boundaries/`, `public/datasets/economy/`).
*   **Action:** Update `utils/geojsonLoader.ts` to actually `fetch()` these URLs over the network instead of checking a local Map/Object.
*   **Benefit:** This creates a true "Static Site" architecture where data is loaded on demand, ensuring fast initial load times.

#### **Phase 2: Content Migration (The "Manual" Backend)**
*   **Action:** Use the **Admin Console > Dataset Upload** tool you built. Load your real Shapefiles/GeoJSONs into it.
*   **Action:** Take the **JSON Output** generated by the tool and paste it into `types.ts` (or split this out into a dedicated `data/catalog.json` file that is imported).
*   **Action:** Place the corresponding GeoJSON files into the `public/` folder.
*   **Action:** Replace all `picsum` URLs with real screenshots stored in `public/images/`.

#### **Phase 3: Hardening & Launch**
*   **Action:** Set `NODE_ENV=production` in your build pipeline.
*   **Action:** Configure `robots.txt` to disallow the Admin views (already done in code, ensure file is copied).
*   **Action:** Deploy to a static host (Vercel/Netlify).
*   **Action:** Test the "Contact" forms. Currently, `ReportIssuePage` mocks the submission. You should integrate a service like **Formspree** or **EmailJS** so you actually receive these emails without needing a backend.

### **Conclusion**
The application code is high quality, modular, and adheres to strict protocols. The only thing missing is the **physical separation of code and data**. Once you move the map data out of the JavaScript bundle and into static files, this application is ready to be the new face of Guynode.
